codeReview.summary: Clean, correct implementation of input validation for all 4 IPC handlers that accept renderer parameters. The validation logic is placed at the top of each handler (before any side effects), uses idiomatic Node.js patterns (new URL(), path.resolve(), regex, typeof), and consistently returns { success: false, error: <string> } for all rejection paths. No exceptions are thrown on invalid input — the try/catch in open-external-url and open-resource catches filesystem errors but validation failures return before reaching any dangerous operation. The /* global URL */ directive correctly addresses ESLint's no-undef rule for the browser-provided URL constructor.

issues.description: The open-video-window handler destructures its parameter as { videoId, moduleId: _moduleId } which means a compromised renderer could send arbitrary extra properties in the object. The handler correctly validates videoId but does not validate moduleId (which is only used as a local variable alias _moduleId, so it's harmless). However, the destructuring pattern itself is safe since it only extracts known fields — no prototype pollution or injection risk. Noting for completeness only.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The security-worker skill's IPC Input Validation Pattern example shows validation inside a try/catch with the URL parsing as the first action, but the actual implementation for open-external-url uses a different (and arguably better) pattern: it checks typeof first, then parses URL inside the try block alongside shell.openExternal. This means a malformed URL string (which causes new URL() to throw) is caught by the existing catch block rather than a dedicated validation check. Both patterns work correctly, but the worker's approach relies on the generic catch for malformed URLs. The skill's example pattern is slightly more explicit about catching parse failures separately.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Skill SKILL.md shows: 'try { const parsed = new URL(param); ... } catch { return { success: false, error: "Invalid URL" }; }'. Actual implementation at main.js:255-270 wraps both validation and shell.openExternal in one try/catch, with malformed URLs caught by the generic error handler that returns 'Invalid URL: <error.message>'.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker correctly added /* global URL */ at the top of main.js to satisfy ESLint's no-undef rule for the browser URL constructor. This is a good pattern for Electron main process files that use browser globals. AGENTS.md does not currently mention this ESLint directive convention.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: main.js:1 — '/* global URL */' added alongside existing require() imports. This was necessary because ESLint's env:node setting doesn't include URL as a global, but Electron's main process has access to it via V8.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The architecture.md was already updated to reflect the IPC validation additions (noted under 'Electron IPC' section: 'All IPC handlers validate inputs', 'Path traversal prevention', 'URL protocol whitelist'). This appears to have been done as part of the mission infrastructure setup rather than by this worker, which is good practice.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: .factory/library/architecture.md — Electron IPC section documents all three validation patterns implemented in this feature.

summary: The ipc-input-validation feature is correctly implemented. All 4 IPC handlers that accept renderer parameters (open-external-url, open-resource, open-video-window, close-video-window) now validate inputs before processing. The validation patterns are security-appropriate: URL protocol whitelisting via new URL() parsing, path traversal prevention via path.resolve() + startsWith(), YouTube video ID regex validation, and typeof number check. All rejections return { success: false, error: <string> } without throwing. The 426 existing tests pass and lint is clean. Assertions VAL-IPC-001 through VAL-IPC-005 are all fulfilled. One non-blocking observation about the skill's example pattern vs actual implementation (both are correct).
